data.title: Broadcom: Why AI Vibe-Coding Is Not A Threat To Its Infrastructure Software Segment
data.description:
Broadcom Inc. (AVGO) investors might have thought recent and very large cap-ex announcements by customers Meta Platforms (META) and Alphabet/Google (GOOG) would have caused a rebound in what has been a pretty brutal and persistent sell-off that began after last quarter's earnings report. Then came reports that HBM (high bandwidth memory) prices have jumped and could stay higher for another year or two. Now, the latest threat to Broadcom appears to be the "SaaSpocalypse" narrative wherein AI vibe-coding will threaten its high-margin Infrastructure Software Segment.

Specifically, Anthropic (ANTHRO) has prioritized high-value AI coding tools, and the company's latest Claude plugins have seemingly been heat-seeking missiles to destroy vulnerable software companies across the sector. Indeed, over the past month, this narrative has erased hundreds of billions in the market-cap of companies like Microsoft (MSFT), ETFs like the iShares Expanded Tech Software ETF (IGV), and even Broadcom (see chart below). Today, I'll explain why this narrative is irrational as it pertains to Broadcom's Infrastructure Software Segment and why I am upgrading Broadcom to a Strong Buy.

Investment Thesis

My followers and Seeking Alpha investors in general are likely very familiar with the investment thesis supporting Broadcom: it's because Broadcom has what I have termed the "AI Trifecta":

  • A leading provider of cutting-edge high-speed networking equipment.
  • A leading provider of optimized XPU compute engines for AI data centers.
  • A leading provider of high-margin Infrastructure Software including VMware virtualization software that includes full-stack support for Nvidia GPUs, AMD GPUs, and – of course – Broadcom's XPUs, offering VMware customers tremendous value-added flexibility.

These business lines, combined with a top-flight management team led by CEO Hock Tan, have created a free-cash-flow machine that has delivered dividend increases of 10% annually.

Further, it's very important to understand that Broadcom is not taking on a massive debt load to invest in AI. In fact, it's just the opposite. As I reported in my widely read Seeking Alpha article, "Broadcom: What So Many Analysts And Investors Got Wrong – Buy The Dip," Broadcom actually reduced FY 2025 year-end long-term debt by $4.3 billion while total long-term liabilities were reduced by $8.2 billion. And that is exactly why I called out any "analysts" comparisons of Broadcom to Oracle as being irrational and irresponsible.

All that being the case, one would have thought recent announcements by two of Broadcom's biggest XPU, TPU, and networking customers – META and Google – to increase 2026 cap-ex spending to midpoints of $125 billion and $180 billion, respectively, would have significantly boosted AVGO shares. Yet, despite those cap-ex announcements being wildly bullish for Broadcom (at least in my opinion), the shares did not respond (at least not yet). In addition, Broadcom is one of Taiwan Semiconductor's (TSM) largest customers. Yet despite a report that TSMC's January revenue jumped a whopping 37% YoY, Broadcom's stock hardly budged.

The likely and latest reason for Broadcom stock's relatively weak performance despite the very bullish fundamental news flow is that Wall Street apparently has irrationally attached yet another false narrative to Broadcom: the previously mentioned "SaaSpocalypse." That's probably because Broadcom has intentionally grown its Infrastructure Software Segment with the recent strategic acquisition of VMware such that in FY 2025 the segment generated $27 billion in revenue (42% of total revenue), a gross margin of 93% (up two full percentage points YOY), and actually grew faster (26%) than the Semiconductor Segment (22%):

So, clearly, Broadcom's Infrastructure Software Segment is key to the company's success moving forward. There is no doubt about that. So, today, I will explain why the "SaaSpocalypse" narrative does not apply to Broadcom's software operations - at least not in the very negative way the market is apparently expecting.

AI Vibe-Coding

As you all know, all the leading AI large language models, or LLMs, have the ability to generate code. In my opinion, there is no doubt AI vibe-coding is going to be a huge productivity enhancer and that AI code generation is certainly going to harvest low-hanging fruit throughout the software sector.

For example, from its beginning, Anthropic has strategically focused on AI code generation. Earlier this month, Anthropic released new AI tools, in the form of Claude plugins, that were specifically tailored for the sales, finance, and legal professions. The announcement on 'X' caused panic selling across a number of related companies.

Last week privately held financial software provider Altruist Corp. launched an AI tool that creates tax strategies for clients. That caused a deep selloff in stocks like Charles Schwab Corp. (SCHW), Raymond James Financial (RJF), and Stifel Financial (SF).

So there is no doubt AI vibe-coding is disrupting the software sector – whether totally rational or not doesn't matter when the mere perception drives a stock down 8–10% in a day. But it is my opinion that some software companies are much more vulnerable than others. In my opinion, Broadcom's Infrastructure Software Segment has a wide moat and will continue to thrive. Here's why.

The Network Is The Computer

I have discussed this topic in some of my prior articles on Broadcom and highlighted the fact that as thousands of GPUs, XPUs, and TPUs are clustered together, the ability to keep them fed with the low-latency, high-bandwidth data required for these high-performance compute engines to function at full speed is absolutely critical to the overall success of a data center's ability to monetize AI.

Consider the following topology of a hypothetical data center architecture:

This slide was taken from a Broadcom Networking presentation on YouTube, which I encourage all investors to watch it.

This slide shows 4 data centers that, in a distributed computing architecture, need to communicate with each other and share data across the compute engines within and outside each data center. This is achieved through what the industry is calling a "Scale-Out Networking Interconnect." It is important to note that this is not a simple hardware interconnection problem – networking is also a very complex software-driven issue as well.

That is even more the case given that customers want to mix and match their choices of compute engines within each data center. Some companies need GPUs for LLM training as well as optimized XPUs for inference, and all companies would want second sources for compute engines for three reasons: to ensure access to enough compute capacity, to reduce costs, and to strengthen their negotiating positions with compute engine providers.

So, inside the data center diagram above, consider the following "Scale-Up" compute engine configuration (this slide, which was also taken from the previously referenced Broadcom presentation):

Note the three compute engines, A, B, and C, in the upper left-hand corner. "A" might be an Nvidia GPU, "B" might be a Broadcom XPU, and "C" might well be a Google TPU. All three compute engines need to (efficiently) run the full stack by the customer's chosen Infrastructure Software package. So, as you can see, not only are the data center interconnections heavily dependent on the networking technology, architecture, and Infrastructure Software, so too are the internal components of each leaf in the rack and each rack in the system.

Further, consider the following slide taken from a different Broadcom presentation given at the April 2024 OCP Regional Summit:

This slide refers to results from a study by META that shows just how important networking input/output is for AI recommendation workloads. What the results show is that networking was the bottleneck roughly 35% of the time. What this means is that those high-cost GPUs were idle 35% of the time because they were waiting on data, waiting to get rid of data, or on backups or error recovery processes.

One of the advantages of implementing Broadcom's networking equipment, and one reason Broadcom always seems to be one step ahead of the competition (if not two ...), is because of decades of experience. Broadcom arguably has the best networking development system on the planet – from both a hardware and software perspective. That is, Broadcom has a deep set of verification and validation tests it can run on any networking architecture to make sure the system functions properly and runs securely and with high performance.

Clearly then, networking optimization and infrastructure software is critical from both a functional and a performance perspective, right? And both are key to optimizing AI monetization.

Now, with that as background, pretend you are an IT executive at a Fortune 500 company that has decided to spend an estimated $4 billion on a 100,000 GPU cluster for an on-premises private cloud data center. The vast majority of those funds are going to be spent on expensive AI/ML GPUs, XPUs, and TPUs - with a few hundred million left over for networking equipment and infrastructure software – including required support for the following:

Now, ask yourself a simple question:

Should I risk my company's entire investment (and likely my job ...) on unproven AI vibe-coding solutions or should I simply stick with Broadcom's proven VMware Infrastructure Software solutions?

While the answer should be obvious, consider the following before answering: last June, Broadcom released VMware Cloud Foundation (VCF) 9.0 that included several significant upgrades for private cloud operations:

  • Unified Operations and Experience.
  • Enhanced Performance and Efficiency.
  • Improved Networking and Security.

The enhanced Performance and Efficiency initiatives included (but weren't limited to) the following:

  1. "Advanced NVMe Memory Tiering: VCF 9.0 allows extending the memory pool with NVMe, treating fast NVMe devices as a lower-cost, second-tier memory. This frees up DRAM for active workloads, potentially leading to significant server consolidation by running more VMs or containers per host" and
  2. "New kernel optimizations can deliver up to 3x faster data speeds, lower latency, and reduced CPU overhead, especially beneficial for AI/ML workloads."

As you can see by these enhancements, VMware continues to make improvements to optimize networking performance. That's because Broadcom understands it's not just GPU performance, or networking performance, or software performance that matters - it's the overall system performance that counts. And Broadcom has been working for over a decade with partners like Google and META on optimizing the performance of every piece of the system.

International Data Corporation's ("IDC") conclusion about VCF 9.0:

Broadcom, through VMware, is strategically positioning VCF 9.0 to intensify competition within the hybrid cloud market, directly challenging hyperscalers by offering a unified, cost-optimized private/hybrid stack that addresses specific enterprise pain points. This move suggests a rebalancing of power in the cloud ecosystem, where the capabilities and economic advantages of modern private cloud solutions are gaining renewed prominence.

Risks

Now, could some of Broadcom's legacy mainframe and enterprise software solutions, like Symantec, be negatively impacted by AI vibe-coding? Perhaps ... but even that is not a given considering the large enterprise-level risks associated with making a huge change like that. But even if some revenue were to roll off those legacy software products as contracts expire, in the big picture, revenue growth in VMware AI Infrastructure Software sales will likely more than make up for them. And VCF is where the real action is going forward.

Summary & Conclusion

Due to the factors discussed above, Broadcom's VMware Enterprise Software solutions have a much wider moat as compared to the average everyday SaaS-based software company. That being the case, current concerns about AI vibe-coding taking a significant chunk out of Broadcom's Infrastructure Software Segment are, in my opinion, irrational.

Broadcom's guidance for Q1 FY 2026 was for revenue of ~$19.1 billion - an increase of 28% YoY. When the company releases its Q1 results after the market closes on March 4th, I expect they will be top and bottom line beats. That's not only because Broadcom has a history of conservative guidance that under-promises and over-delivers, but also because all the sector's news flow since last quarter has been very bullish.

As a result, and combined with recent and continued weakness in Broadcom's stock price despite very strong fundamental news from META, Google, and TSMC, I am upgrading Broadcom to a STRONG BUY.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Five9: Reset Valuation And New CEO Create Opportunity
data.description:
Five9, Inc. (FIVN) has given me the kind of setup I usually pay attention to. I upgraded it to a Buy in mid-October, and the stock is down nearly 30% since then. That drop does not automatically mean my call was wrong, but it definitely raises the standard for what I want to see next.

I think the timing is also perfect for a check-in. Five9 reports its Q4 and full-year FY25 results on February 19, 2026, so this is the window where we see expectations hardening and narratives start to form.

For me, though, the bigger wrinkle is leadership. Five9 appointed Amit Mathradas as CEO in December, and he stepped in on February 2, 2026. That CEO transition will affect how investors price uncertainty around the company, even if the underlying business stays the same.

First, though, I will review the company’s results from Q3 2025.

Q3 FY25 Results: Solid Execution And Capital Allocation

Before getting ahead of ourselves, it’s still worth grounding this discussion in what Five9 actually delivered in Q3.

Metric

Those results reinforce a few things I already believed about Five9’s execution. Its subscription mix is improving, margins are expanding in a disciplined way, and cash generation is real. In addition to that, we can see that AI is contributing meaningfully to growth, particularly at the enterprise level.

Capital allocation was also positive. Management authorized a $150 million share repurchase program and followed it up with a $50 million accelerated share repurchase that should settle at the end of Q1 FY26. The latter isn’t done yet, and we should get an update on it during the company’s earnings call next week. In my opinion, these moves signal that management is confident in free cash flow durability. Besides, they provide some downside support after the stock’s big selloff over the last few months.

All things considered, I don’t think Q3 or the ASR has reset the narrative. The company’s fundamentals are stabilizing and gradually improving, but the stock’s pullback since October suggests that valuation and uncertainty have been the dominant forces.

What I Expect From Q4’s Results

Mr. Mathradas won’t have anything to do with Five9's performance in the last quarter, but he’ll present the results. The first thing I’m looking at is revenue quality. The word from management in Q3 was revenue in the range of $295 to $301 million, which is a fairly modest step up from Q3. I think they’ll hit the target, but the more important question is how much of the growth comes from core subscription revenue and enterprise customers. I want to see Five9 continue to lean into the durable spend, not just one-off usage or end-of-year timing benefits.

In Q3, we also saw that the company can sustain an adjusted EBITDA margin in the mid-20s, seeing as that was the third time in four quarters it recorded a figure in that range.

I’d like that discipline to hold in Q4, even as the business is investing in AI and go-to-market. At the same time, I wouldn’t be surprised if it slips a bit, but that will raise questions about how balanced the next phase really is.

AI monetization is the next thing. Five9 is growing its enterprise AI revenue fairly quickly, and the attach rates on the larger deals are very encouraging. That’s great, but I’m less interested in the growth percentage and more interested in whether it is consistently contributing to bookings, expansion, and retention. It is crucial that the company deepen its customer relationships because revenue compounding starts from existing clients.

The Leadership Transition

Five9 has handed the CEO role to Amit Mathradas at a time when it’s still in a bit of flux. I can’t say he’s walking into a full-blown turnaround, but he’s not exactly inheriting a runaway growth story either.

I like his background because he’s a builder, according to my research. I’ll run through a few of his most recent roles, so forgive me if I’m a bit terse. He was CEO at Nintex, where he ran a product-led software company that was heavy on AI and automation, among other things. Before that, he was the President and COO at Avalara, where he scaled a complex SaaS business through a long growth phase and into an eventual take-private transaction. He’s also held senior executive and operational roles at PayPal and Dell.

I think that profile fits where Five9 is at this point, and I don’t expect him to blow up what’s already working. There are things that I would appreciate some more color and context around, though. For example, I’d like to hear answers to questions like:

  • Where does AI actually drive incremental revenue versus just improve retention?

  • How aggressive should the company be on reinvestment now that margins have room?

  • And how does Five9 position itself in comparison to peers like NICE without chasing scale for its own sake?

I know the market usually treats CEO transitions like this as an overhang, and I understand why. There are several unknowns, especially when you bring in an outsider, and he’ll need time to find his feet and settle in. In this case, though, I see more opportunity than risk. Mathradas is inheriting a cleaner balance sheet, good cash flow, and a platform that’s already gaining ground with enterprise customers. That gives him a lot of optionality.

I believe that the earnings call next week will set the tone for how we view his leadership, especially over the next few quarters. I’ll pay close attention to his tone, what he has to say about capital allocation decisions, and how explicitly management links AI investment to its revenue outcomes.

Valuation: Expectations Have Reset, But They’re Not Trivial

Five9 Valuation Snapshot (pre-Q4)

Metric

Approx. value

Share price

~$16

Market cap

~$1.3B

Net debt

~$121M

Enterprise value

~$1.42B

TTM revenue

~$1.13B

FY25 revenue guide

~$1.14–1.15B

EV/TTM sales

~1.3x

EV/FY25 sales

~1.25x

Adj. EBITDA margin (Q3)

~25%

EV/EBITDA (TTM)

~14x

At the current price, I think the valuation lines up reasonably well with where the company is at, but there’s a wide gap to where it should be. I’ve compared Five9 to competitors like NICE and RingCentral in my prior articles, and it is trading at an even bigger discount to them now.

This is a business that is delivering high single-digit revenue growth while improving its recurring revenue mix and margins. It is worth more than 1.3x sales by any comparable standards, and I believe the market will correct the valuation in due course.

Then, there’s the ASR arrangement with JPMorgan Chase. I don’t think the $50 million ASR will move these multiples dramatically on its own, but it is important at the margin. For one, it offsets any dilution, and it supports EPS while telling the broader market that management is comfortable allocating capital at these prices. If we take that in conjunction with the company’s improving cash flow, I think this puts a floor under the valuation while we wait and see how the leadership transition plays out.

The stock isn’t cheap in an absolute sense of the word, because at 1.3x, I’m assuming that Five9 will hold its current margin profile and continue growing its subscription mix. Those are reasonable assumptions, but they can also fail to materialize as expected, and that doesn’t leave much cushion at today’s price.

The Risks

I touched on some of the risks associated with Five9 at different points in this article, but I’ll list them again here.

We have the most straightforward one, the CEO transition. It’s only really a risk if we see the company’s priorities shift suddenly or its cost discipline evaporate, neither of which is particularly likely.

Furthermore, topline growth can stall, and given that it’s only in the high single-digit/low teens range at this point, I don’t think that will do the margins any good. They’re already tight as is, even though they’re recovering, so it makes sense to watch out for this.

My last point relates to AI monetization. Basically, we need to see AI translating into even more meaningful revenue lift. That way, I can make a case for reacceleration and the stock rerating.

I’d Still Recommend A Buy

The overall outlook for Five9 is good, in my opinion. It has tailwinds in its business, and it is doing well on an operational and financial level. Today’s valuation has more to do with some leadership uncertainty and general softness in the software market for 2026, but that’s about it.

It’s a solid company with an undervalued stock price, and the recent pullback should work in favor of long-term buyers, especially if Q4 and FY25 results come in as expected.

data.title: Alnylam Hit Hard As Investors Forced To Revise Future Profit Expectations
data.description:
It’s been a rough go for Alnylam Pharmaceuticals (ALNY) since October 2025, with the shares down almost 40% from an all-time high. While it is typically clinical failures that hit biotechs hard, Alnylam is in that awkward biopharma adolescence where investors care more and more about the actual financials. To that end, worries about dynamics in the core TTR market and future profitability loom larger than any concerns about the clinical pipeline.

Alnylam shares are down another 20% since my last update, as the concerns I raised about guidance have come to pass.

I don’t believe there are long-standing issues with the TTR market that are going to materially reduce Alnylam’s long-term earnings. I also don’t think that the company’s decision to continue reinvesting in the business will be a bad thing for long-term shareholders, but I do acknowledge the impact it has on near-term sentiment. For patient investors, this is a name to consider once again, but it may take a while for sentiment to come back around and rerate the shares higher again.

A Pre-Announced Miss

Alnylam basically unveiled fourth quarter results at a sell-side conference earlier in January, and the results reported on February 12 basically mirrored that preview. Unfortunately, those weak trends observed in prescription data and in commentary from rivals like Pfizer (PFE) did play out, as the company missed expectations for product revenue and the TTR franchise in particular.

Revenue rose 85% year over year and declined 12% sequentially, missing expectations by 6%. Product revenue rose 121% YoY and 17% QoQ, missing by less than 3%. The TTR franchise saw 151% YoY and 19% QoQ growth, with Amvuttra growing to 96% of the business. Although prescription trends were weaker in the quarter, it seems to largely just be “noise,” with no underlying changes in prescribing and Amvuttra continuing to gain share in the market.

Alnylam’s two other approved drugs had mixed, but generally decent, results. Givlaari revenue rose 34%, beating by 9%. Oxlumo rose 14% but missed by 7%. Collectively, these drugs contribute less than 14% of product revenue and a little over 12% of total revenue.

Collaboration and royalty revenue declined 28%, and while this was a bigger miss, the lack of disclosure around the timing and other details of collaboration payments can make it a hard-to-model figure. Collaboration revenue declined 62%, largely due to the lack of recurrence payments from Regeneron (REGN) and Novartis (NVS) in the year-ago quarter.

Royalty revenue was up 85%, with Novartis seeing 46% YoY growth in Leqvio and $1.2B in revenue for the full year. Sanofi (SNY) reported EUR 4M in revenue for Qfitlia, steady with the prior quarter.

Gross margin declined 410 bp to 73.1%, with a negative impact from increased Amvuttra sales due to the higher royalty requirements (Alnylam pays royalties to Sanofi and Royalty Pharma (RPRX). SG&A spending was up 24%, and R&D spending rose 31%, but the company did report $203M in non-GAAP operating income (or an 18.5% operating margin).

Guidance Maintained, But There Are Issues

While Alnylam guided to weaker-than-expected Q4 TTR sales, it also guided to stronger 2026 results. Management is looking for 83% YoY growth at the midpoint, or $4.55B in revenue, and that’s comfortably above the prior $4.28B expectation. Guidance for 10% growth in the rare disease business (Givlaari and Oxlumo) was less impressive, but not so surprising.

While full-year TTR franchise guidance was good, there are nevertheless ongoing concerns.

To start with, Q1 sales are likely going to be weaker. There will be fewer selling days in the quarter (about two weeks’ worth), and the need to go through insurance reauthorizations will impact treatment volumes. There’s also roughly mid-single-digit price erosion from changes in gross-to-net pricing through agreements with insurance payors.

This seasonality is not unusual, but that doesn’t mean that the Street likes it. Moreover, I think the Street is worried about TTR prescribing trends, particularly with Pfizer preparing for its drug to go generic in the not-too-distant future. I expect prescriptions to pick up as the year goes on, and I expect Amvuttra to continue gaining share, but this is a “show me” story, particularly with 2027 read-outs from potentially competitive programs from AstraZeneca (AZN).

Another emerging issue for valuation and sentiment is the company’s margin trajectory. Management guided to roughly 15% higher than expected opex for FY 2026, and this follows guidance from that sell-side conference that disappointed investors with respect to future margins.

Management has made it clear that they see a major runway of potential treatments with their core technology (forecasting long-term annualized revenue growth of over 25%), and they’re going to spend to facilitate that. Part of management’s “Alnylam 2030” guidance included adjusted operating margin of +30% – well below the 45%-50% that had been in some sell-side models.

Simply put, Alnylam is not looking to economize on R&D, and when you consider that there are now over 20 compounds in clinical trials and many more in preclinical development across a range of anatomical targets, it’s going to take money to support this. I agree with this decision, and I think it will build significant long-term value, but the reality is that institutional investors rarely invest with long-term timelines, and management is clearly stating that they are going to spend today for more growth tomorrow.

A Potential "Sleeper Year" For Clinical Data

All in all, 2026 is not looking like a clinically heavy year, as major programs like nucresiran, zilebesiran, and cemdisiran aren’t likely to have meaningful clinical updates from pivotal clinical programs.

That doesn’t mean that the data cupboard will be bare, though. Alnylam expects to present data from three early-stage, high-potential programs later this year – Phase I/II results from ALN-6400 in HHT (a rare bleeding disorder), Phase I from ALN-HTT02 in Huntington’s Disease, and Phase I ALN-2232 in obesity.

The latter surprised me (I thought it would be a 2027 read-out), and given the high level of investor interest in obesity, this could be an attention-grabbing release, particularly given that Alnylam’s early-stage results usually translate well into later-stage studies. ALN-2232 targets ACVR1C/ALK-7, which suppresses lipolysis and promotes fat storage.

In knockout mice studies, blocking ACVR1C makes the mice more resistant to diet-induced obesity and improves insulin sensitivity. This target doesn’t impact appetite directly, but it does seem to “mobilize” fat when caloric intake is reduced, and it’s plausible that this drug could be particularly effective in combination with exercise. On its own, I don’t think this will be a blockbuster for weight reduction, but I could see it being a very effective combo/adjunctive treatment.

As for the other two, I think the opportunities in Huntington’s are pretty well understood. It’s a rare disease, but one where there is no cure or effective means of slowing the disease beyond treating some of the dyskinetic effects. ALN-6400 could be more of a wake-up call; HHT isn’t that common (maybe 150,000 patients in the U.S. and EU), but it is serious and has significant quality of life issues, and efficacy here could support robust pricing (multiple billions of dollars of potential peak revenue).

The Outlook

Because of where Alnylam is now, I’m starting to use a hybrid model that combines my typical peak revenue-driven model with a classical discounted cash flow model. My fair value is going down, in part due to higher share count (which impacts both models), higher-for-longer opex spending, and more uncertainty around the near-term revenue outlook for the TTR franchise.

I now see fair value around $456 (versus almost $500 before), with the TTR franchise still making up roughly 70% of the value. Zilebesiran is the next-most important contributor at around $23/share, with mivelsiran (around $10/share), ALN-6400 ($7/share), and cemdisiran ($6/share) also contributing. Partnered drugs add another $34/share, and the two rare disease drugs contribute more than $21/share. Strong clinical data from ALN-6400, ALN-HTT02, and ALN-2232, even given that it would be early stage, could potentially add some meaningful incremental value.

The Bottom Line

When I say that it’s often difficult or scary to “buy the dip,” this is the sort of situation I mean. Investors are understandably worried about near-term dynamics in the TTR market and the longer-term implications of ALNY management’s spending plans. I don’t dismiss either as legitimate concerns, but I think the market reaction is overdone, and I think this is a high-quality biotech platform worth considering today.

data.title: Applied Materials: Little Opportunity Left After A Monstrous Run
data.description:
Back in September 2025, I downgraded Applied Materials, Inc. (AMAT) from a buy to a hold rating as I cited soft guidance and China risks as justifications for a modestly low valuation. In hindsight, this could have been one of my worst decisions of 2025, as the stock has more than doubled since the publication of my update. With earnings having just come out yesterday (Feb 12th), the chart below actually hasn't even updated to reflect the major gains on the current trading day. Today, I'll conduct an analysis to see whether this months-long rally is justified by the fundamentals and whether there is still opportunity at current levels.

In the below analysis, it is shown that Q1 was a decent quarter for the company. While guidance looks quite good and DRAM represents a large opportunity, China risks are still very noteworthy. Even though the outlook is net positive, Applied Materials' stock seems to already be pricing in their bright prospects, and so I believe reiterating my hold rating would be most appropriate right now.

DRAM Takes Center Stage

By now, it is no secret that a memory shortage has taken hold in the tech sector, and the effects could be far-reaching. As companies aim to meet the demands of the market, Applied Materials is in a position to benefit, as their solutions are critical in the production process. In their FY2026 Q1, they reported record DRAM revenue from their Semiconductor Systems segment, and so it is quite clear that they are already seeing significant tailwinds.

Moving forward, Applied Materials has ambitions to accelerate this momentum. As you can see in the above infographic, Viva Radical Treatment and Sym3 Z Magnum Etch are set for Q2 launches. These new solutions should provide another boost to their already booming DRAM business, and so I would say that the company is well-positioned to continue benefitting from the buildout of AI infrastructure.

Q1 Earnings Review

While it was likely their forward guidance that stole the show, let's start the analysis of the financials with a look at their Q1 top-line results. Applied Materials generated total revenues of $7.01 billion, and that actually represented a 2% decline YoY. While that seems a bit disappointing after a Q4 YoY growth rate of 4%, note that they were still able to beat expectations by a wide margin of $132.23 million, and so things definitely could have been worse. Overall, while the growth deceleration hints at slowing business momentum, their financial outlook may have something to say about that later.

Above, you can see that AGS saw resilient growth, and in my view, that isn't too surprising, as existing clients are essentially locked in. As for Semiconductor Systems, despite the DRAM strength, the decline in revenues is a slight concern. While I'll have more to say about their China business later, note that Applied Materials saw a 7% revenue decline in this market. Therefore, it seems that geopolitical tensions between the U.S. and China are headwinds for the company. As a whole, I would say Q1 was a decent but unspectacular quarter for their overall business activity.

In terms of margins, there were some mixed results. For non-GAAP gross margin, Applied Materials saw 20 basis points of expansion after improvements in both of their segments. This is an indication of continued strong pricing power and good management of their costs.

For operating margin, in GAAP terms, there was a large hit due to a legal settlement. In adjusted terms, there was still some weakness, as there was a 60 basis point contraction, and that demonstrates a bit of deterioration in efficiency. However, on their income statement, you can see that R&D expenses increased more than marketing and selling expenses, while general and administrative expenses actually dropped YoY. Therefore, a significant driver of the operating margin erosion is from continued investments in innovation. Regular readers of my articles know that I usually don't mind a large R&D budget since it leads to better future growth. This applies to Applied Materials as well. Product launches like the ones discussed earlier wouldn't be possible without R&D.

On the bottom line, profitability actually fared quite well considering the revenue decline. Adjusted EPS was unchanged YoY, and while that doesn't sound exciting, it is definitely better than the decline seen in Q4. Furthermore, they beat analyst expectations by a good margin of $0.17. I'll get to their guidance in a little bit, but you can see above that consensus estimates call for a good deal of bottom line growth acceleration moving forward, and so it really does seem that the outlook is the key driver of the stock's post-earnings gains.

Cash Is King

At the end of the day, cash flows are one of the best indicators of a healthy business, and Applied Materials did not disappoint in their Q1. For operating cash flows, they saw an 82% jump YoY while FCFs soared by a whopping 91%. This is very impressive and shows that cash generation has been robust despite the rather sluggish bottom line growth rate. While strong cash flows are obviously encouraging, note that capital returns to shareholders actually dropped significantly YoY.

Just recently, Applied Materials was mentioned on Seeking Alpha's list of large-cap stocks with the highest dividend growth grade and their list of large-cap stocks with the highest dividend safety grade. I believe this is justified as their dividend was raised significantly after FY2025 Q2, and FCFs are more than enough for them to pay out dividends consistently. However, note that share repurchases have dropped sharply YoY from $1.318 billion to just $337 million. This could be an indication of cautiousness or a less attractive stock price, and so investors should have that in mind.

Outlook

As promised, let's take a look at their forward guidance for Q2. Applied Materials is expecting to see total revenues of around $7.65 billion. This implies a YoY growth rate of 8%. Therefore, after the top line decline in Q1, the company seems to be getting back on the right track in terms of business activity.

For gross margin, their expectations of 49.3% would be an improvement from 49.2% in the year-ago quarter, and so cost control is likely to remain solid. As for operating expenses, their projection of $1.415 billion would be an 8% increase. Since this is basically the same as their expected top-line growth rate, the overall efficiency of their business is set to be strong.

Lastly, for the bottom line, their midpoint of $2.64 represents a 10% increase. This would be a considerable improvement after nearly no growth in Q1. As a whole, I consider Applied Materials' guidance to be robust and note that both top and bottom line guidance was above analyst consensus as a sign that the outlook is looking good.

China Risks Remain

Applied Materials exposure to China remains very significant, and political risks are noteworthy. In Q1, 27% of their sales were in the Chinese market, and so pressures by both U.S. and Chinese regulators could create headwinds.

On the U.S. side, the legal settlement that I mentioned in the margin discussion earlier was a result of a U.S. export violation that happened between November 2020 and July 2022. While this is now history, it does suggest that the U.S. Government continues to be very cautious over semiconductor dealings with China.

On the Chinese side of things, at the end of December, it was reported that regulators there will require chipmakers to use at least 50% local equipment. Therefore, not only is the U.S. restricting sales, but China also seems to be on the path of reducing reliance on American chipmaking solutions. With a good part of their sales still coming from the Chinese market, investors should keep in mind that the company is somewhat at the mercy of political tensions between the world's top two economies.

Valuation

As you can see in the table above, Applied Materials is trading at a premium valuation relative to the IT sector and relative to historical averages on most measures. I believe that little opportunity remains for investors at current levels.

Today, it was shown that Q1 was a decent quarter for Applied Materials but nothing special. Cash flows were strong, but the drop in share repurchases may be indicative of an unattractive stock price. As for the outlook, guidance for Q2 is quite strong with DRAM likely to be a notable driver of growth. However, as outlined above, political risks are real as Applied Materials' business still has significant exposure to the Chinese market.

While I would say that the prospects for Applied Materials are net positive right now, I also believe that the premium valuation at which the stock is trading makes the risk/reward unattractive for entry right now.

Conclusion

As stated at the beginning, in hindsight, my downgrade of Applied Materials to a hold was likely a mistake. However, I believe it would be unwise to call the stock a buy now. As stated above, despite resilient Q1 results and DRAM opportunities, there are also China risks. With the valuation being at a premium level on most measures, I believe that the stock already prices in a net positive outlook for the company. With that being the case, I have decided to maintain my hold rating on Applied Materials stock.

data.title: Micron: Institutional Money Can End The Party Abruptly
data.description:
MU Stock: Institutions Have Been Trimming Their Shares

I last analyzed Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) on Jan 11 with an article titled “Micron: Control Your FOMO; Don't Bet Against Insiders Or Professionals.” The article was promoted by insider activities and the transactions of super-investors, and it rated the stock as a Hold. Since then, a few new catalysts have emerged around this memory chip leader. In the remainder of this article, I will focus on the top 3 on my radar: the latest movement of institutional money, the change in put/call ratio, and also the new AI CAPEX plan announced by key hyperscalers for 2026 in their FQ4 earning reports.

An examination of these new catalysts has left me with two takeaways. First, these developments have led me to see that MU’s current bull run hangs in a precarious balance. Many factors (such as a further trimming from institutional investors) could destroy this balance and end the bull run. Second, this precarious balance is also creating unusually higher implied volatility (IV), making the use of covered put options a viable hedging strategy.

With this overview, let me start with the first catalyst, the changes in institutional ownership. As shown in the next chart provided by Fintel, since 2021, Micron’s shares owned by institutions have been on an overall downward trend till around late 2024 and 2025. After that, institutions showed an increase in their appetite, mostly driven by the artificial intelligence boom and demand for High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM) in my model. As you all know, such demand has also resulted in a fantastic bull run of MU share prices.

However, in the later part of 2025, institutional investors have started unwinding their ownership at least in the past 3 months, as seen. Despite their trimming, MU shares are still predominantly owned by institutional investors. Currently, according to Fintel statistics, these investors collectively own about 1.0 billion MU shares, almost 89% of its total outstanding shares. As such, even a relatively further trimming could result in a large sell pressure.

And I do see several reasons for further trimming, as detailed next.

MU Stock: Market Sentiment is Shifting

Among the many reasons, the one with the most immediate relevance to me is the recent changes in the put-call ratios, or more specifically, the put-call volume ratio and the put-call open interest ratio as shown in the next chart.

I don’t believe many readers follow these ratios based on my past interactions with them. So let me start with my CliffsNotes. The volume ratio, currently at 0.62 for MU, measures the number of put and call contracts traded during a single daily session (i.e., the latest trading day). This number resets every day, and thus a low-volume ratio like 0.62 indicates that on this specific day, traders were aggressively buying more calls than puts, signaling bullish sentiment or active buying pressure on this particular day.

In contrast, the open interest ratio, which stands at 1.25, represents the total number of outstanding contracts that have not yet been closed or exercised. Unlike volume, open interest is a cumulative metric that reflects the total "skin in the game" held by market participants over a longer period. While the volume ratio captures the activity of a single day, open interest provides a broader look at the structural positioning of the market, indicating how much capital is committed to bearish versus bullish outcomes.

With the above background, I rely more on the open interest ratio to gauge the prevailing sentiment. Hence, the current configuration of a high open interest ratio of 1.25 (quite elevated by historical standards, as seen in the chart) alongside a low daily volume ratio of 0.62 sends 2 signals to me: A) a large number of investors are holding puts to either bet on a price drop or hedge existing long positions against a potential reversal, and B) only a small group of investors are leaning towards the bullish in the past few days.

MU Stock: High IV and State of the Cycle

The tension between the two ratios has led to unusually high IV (implied volatilities) for the stock. In the chart below, the top part displays several volatility indicators for MU stock, including an IV of 78.82% and its IV percentile of 97%. This means the stock’s IV has been higher than the current level only 3% of the time in the past year, suggesting that investors have become highly uncertain (and thus also nervous) about significant stock price movements in the near term. Note that the stock’s next earnings event is fast approaching (scheduled for March 25, 2026), and I expect the volatilities to remain elevated till then.

Existing investors could consider the use of covered put options as a hedging strategy. With the unusually high IV, selling covered puts could generate a favorable premium, which helps to cushion downside developments, say a negative surprise from the next earnings report (ER).

Other Risks and Final Thoughts

On the positive side, major hyperscalers have all announced the expansion of their AI CAPEX budget for 2026 in their recent ERs for FQ4, 2025. To wit, according to statistics provided by Goldman Sachs, the top five hyperscalers (AMZN, GOOG, META, MSFT, and ORCL) have drastically exceeded consensus estimates about their AI CAPEX in 2025. As a group, their AI CAPEX rose 64% YOY, compared to an expected growth of “only” 22% from the consensus at the beginning of the year. Looking ahead to 2026, I see continued AI CAPEX investments from these hyperscalers, judging by their FQ4 ER, which could lead to continued demand for MU’s memory chips. As a reflection of this demand, the next chart illustrates the inventory data for MU. To wit, it shows the Days Inventory Outstanding (DIO) for Micron in the past 5 years, from 2022 to the present. As seen, DIO has been trending downward in 2025, and the current reading of 125 days is well below the historical average of 140.

In terms of valuation, the stock’s FWD P/E ratio is certainly attractive. For the fiscal period ending August 2026, the EPS estimate is $33.73 (with a staggering 306% year-over-year growth), translating into a FWD P/E of 12.27x only. Earnings are expected to grow in the next two fiscal years with double-digit rates too, further compressing the P/E ratios into the single-digit range of around 9.52x. However, as detailed in my last article, I suggest potential investors against the reliance on accounting P/E ratios given the highly cyclical nature of the business.

To close, this MU revisit was promoted by 3 new catalysts since my last writing: the latest movement of institutional money, the change in its put/call ratios, and the 2026 AI CAPEX plan announced by key hyperscalers in their FQ4 earnings report. These changes have led me to see a delicate balance of forces that are underpinning the current share prices. Given the ongoing uncertainties and the large institutional ownership, a relatively small re-allocation among these investors could end this balance. And judging by the current put-call ratios and extreme IV, I certainly see good odds for such re-allocation decisions.

data.title: Microsoft: I Like This Price And I Like This Strategy More Than The Stock
data.description:
Article Thesis

I wrote about Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) at the beginning of November 2024, when it was trading at ~$423, and in April 2025, with the stock between $380 and $390. On both occasions, I rated it a Hold, with a fair value estimate only slightly above the stock price.

I usually don’t initiate positions for stocks that are fairly valued. However, in this case, there was (and still is) a very powerful combination that made me interested in a position: a strong economic moat, double-digit revenue growth, very high profitability, and financial flexibility.

Therefore, with an eye on these strengths and with the other on valuation, I didn’t open a full stock position because I couldn’t see the stock rally very much. I opened a more limited bull Put Spread position through options. With a volatile stock, this position brought a profit, although much less than my average gains with this strategy (more about this later).

After closing my Put Spreads, the stock continued its volatile journey, with a top at ~$542 in October and a bottom at ~$394 just recently. With Microsoft trading again around ~$400, I became interested in the position once more, especially since revenue growth estimates are a little higher than last year. Therefore, a business and fair value reassessment is in order to decide my next move.

Business Segments And Economic Moat

Let’s see Microsoft’s recent developments from the last quarter, and let’s reassess their economic moat for each business segment.

Productivity and Business Processes (about 42% of total revenue and with an average growth):

  • Microsoft 365 Commercial Cloud revenue increased 17% YoY, driven by ARPU growth, with strength in E5 and Copilot. With a quasi-monopoly, plus switching costs with Office integrated in many other applications, I reiterate a very strong moat.
  • Microsoft 365 Consumer Cloud revenue increased 29%, again driven by ARPU growth. I reiterate a strong moat, although maybe not as strong as for the more complex commercial applications.
  • LinkedIn revenue increased 11% driven by Marketing Solutions. With ~80% of B2B social media leads originated from LinkedIn and with constantly growing revenues, I see an increasing moat between narrow and strong.
  • Dynamics 365 revenue increased 19%, slightly higher than that of its competitor SAP SE (SAP), about the same as Oracle Fusions’ growth (ORCL), and clearly higher than Workday’s (WDAY), but with the latter having a less comprehensive solution. Combined with very high switching costs for these complex applications, I reiterate a strong moat.

Intelligent Cloud (~40.5% of total revenue and the fastest growing segment):

  • Azure and Other Cloud revenue grew 39%, with demand continuing to exceed available supply. Azure’s growth continues to surpass Amazon's AWS (AMZN), although it was surpassed by Alphabet's Google Cloud (GOOGL). With high switching costs and a powerful ecosystem, I reiterate a strong moat.
  • On-premises Server business revenue increased 2%, but with a one-time benefit from the launch of SQL Server 2025. Although a legacy system, I reiterate a strong moat, with a powerful ecosystem around Windows Server and high switching costs for SQL Server and Visual Studio.

More Personal Computing (~17.5% of total revenue with a slow and volatile growth):

  • Windows OEM and Devices revenue increased 1%, benefiting from Windows 10 end of support. The same strong ecosystem around Windows provides it a strong moat, while I still can’t see a moat for their devices (with declining sales), but this is a negligible piece of pie.
  • Search and News Advertising revenue ex-TAC increased 10%, with a benefit from third-party partnerships normalized. I found it difficult to assign a moat to this sub-segment, but it’s encouraging to see Bing’s market share slightly growing from 3.95% last year to 4.45%, firmly ranking it as the second-largest search engine behind Google. I think there are signs of a narrow economic moat; the market will always try to diversify a little.
  • Xbox Content and Services revenue decreased 5%, confirming its more cyclical nature. I do see a narrow economic moat based on a powerful franchise that triggers subscriptions and in-line game purchases. Like for Devices, I see no moat for Xbox hardware, but again, this is a negligible piece of pie.

Overall, I see a strong moat for ~90% of their business (and growing), a narrow moat for ~10%, and no moat, only a negligible part. I think that we can safely round it to a strong moat, reinforced by a strong ROIC (Return On Invested Capital) of ~19.4%.

An interesting development, which can bring future margin improvements, is the deployment in select data centers of their custom chip Maia. AI is entering an era when inference will have a larger weight than training, and these custom chips are particularly good for inference and specifically designed for in-house optimization, reducing their over-reliance on Nvidia’s GPUs.

Financial Highlights

Overall, Microsoft had a great 17% growth YoY in the last quarter, or 15% in constant currency:

Gross margin is insignificantly down YoY to 68%, due to continued investments in AI infrastructure, partially offset by an increased scale in Microsoft 365 and Azure Cloud. However, with contained expenses, especially SG&A, the operating margin is very slightly up to 47%.

With ~16% revenue growth and a huge ~57% EBITDA margin, Microsoft continues to score an impressive ~74 pts for the Rule of 40. However, if we look at the free cash flow margin, this is significantly down to ~7.2% from ~33% one year before, suddenly dragging Microsoft below the Rule of 40 threshold. While there is, of course, some normal cyclicality in Net Working Capital, by far the main culprit here is the overly aggressive CapEx trend. Let’s say that, as long as “demand exceeds supply” for AI Cloud, this is not a major concern, although it clearly changes the company’s risk profile a little (more about that later):

Looking to the future, commercial RPO (Remaining Performance Obligations) increased to $625B, up 110% YoY with a weighted average duration of approximately 2.5 years. But what scared investors is that approximately 45% of their commercial RPO balance is from OpenAI (OPENAI). And I agree this is clearly an increased risk, although the reaction seems exaggerated (more about that later).

Let’s see more details about their next quarter outlook:

Overall, expected revenue growth is 15%-17%, COGS growth is 22%-23%, and operating expenses growth is 10%-11%. These should bring operating margin slightly down YoY. However, with a “favorable impact from a higher mix of revenue in our Windows OEM and commercial on-prem businesses,” FY '26 operating margin is expected to be up slightly, although I think that’s negligible.

Fair Value

For a relative valuation, it’s difficult to compare it with peers because Microsoft is a unique, diversified business, but we can look at a comparison with Microsoft’s five-year averages:

I look especially at two multiples:

  • PEG Non-GAAP FWD (because it includes both profitability and growth): 1.69x compared to 2.37x implies an undervaluation of ~40%.
  • EV/EBITDA (because it includes both profitability and debt, and this time let’s look at trailing twelve months): 17.33x compared to 23.44x implies an undervaluation of ~35%.

For an absolute valuation, I use a 10-year DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) model. Let’s look first at consensus revenue estimates:

I will use these estimates for the next three years, where we have better visibility based on their outlook, RPO, market trends, etc. But then I find that ~17% growth for Jun’29 a bit stretched. I model a gradually decreasing growth: 14% in 2029, 12% in 2030, 10% in 2031… 6% in 2035.

For operating margin, I model 46% for 2026, under pressure due to this AI infrastructure expansion, then gradually increasing 100 bps/year until 2030, driven by higher scale for Microsoft 365 and Azure, but also by lower expenses with the mass use of their Maia chips. From 2031 to 2035, I model only a minor improvement from 50% to 51%, because they are already close to a maximum scale.

For CapEx, I will model $125B this fiscal year, although they can come above with this overly aggressive trend. Then, I will model a slight increase to $135B for the next two years, followed by a decrease to a bottom of $100B around 2030-2031, driven by lower exposure to external chips and a more balanced supply-demand. I try to model about 2/3 of this CapEx “short-term” (about 6 years depreciation, especially for those GPUs and servers), although I must say that this is difficult in an overall intricate business.

I will model an effective tax rate of 18.5%, between the one from last year and the expected one for Q3’26, and a growth in perpetuity of 3.5%, driven by their business productivity applications, but also by AI and quantum computing.

Finally, I model WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital) = 9.1%, coming from a 9.4% cost of equity (~95% in the capital structure). While this is close to Aswath Damodaran’s 9.64% for “Software (System & Application),” and it’s normal to be a little lower due to its stronger moat, I also use a more qualitative method. I start from ~11%, which I see for a no-moat software company, then:

  • I subtract 1% for their strong economic moat (just a bit more than in my previous model, with a moat seen now in some marginal segments).
  • I subtract 0.5% for their strong profitability.
  • I subtract 0.3% for their financial flexibility (a little less than in my previous model, due to their lower free cash flow, influenced by huge CapEx).
  • I add back 0.2% for their over-dependence on OpenAI (this is new).

Resulting Fair Value is $545, a 36% potential upside from the current price ($401.8 Thursday’s closing price). And we can see relative valuation coming very close to that.

For a sensitivity analysis, let’s further vary WACC and growth in perpetuity, which are probably the most uncertain variables in the model:

Fair Value

WACC=10.1%

WACC=9.1%

WACC=8.1%

Growth=2.5%

$412.5

$584

Growth=3.5%

$453.3

$545

$677.4

Growth=4.5%

$509.1

$631.5

$822.7

We can observe that Microsoft seems fairly valued with more conservative assumptions: WACC = 10.1% and growth in perpetuity = 2.5%.

Risks And Rating

OpenAI was mentioned 16 times during their last earnings call, and for good reasons. 45% reliance on a single customer is a lot, no matter how we put it. And it’s not like it is a deep-pockets customer; we are talking about an unprofitable business. While acknowledging this risk (including it in my WACC), I am far from some other opinions that I saw these days, that OpenAI is a doomed business, unprofitable, and with a lot of debt. How could a business exceeding 10% monthly growth, with more than $800 million ChatGPT users, with expanding tools like Codex, vision/image generation, and others, and with new sources of revenue like ads, be doomed? And a business that grows by triple digits annually and is already profitable is quasi-impossible, unless you are called Nvidia once in a decade. If increasing debt were coupled with significantly lower growth, then I would re-evaluate.

Another risk that I included in my WACC is their huge CapEx. Asked about their RPO duration, management answered that this is, in fact, longer for Azure, which carries a higher CapEx-related risk, therefore reducing this risk:

A lot of the GPUs that we're buying are already contracted for most of their useful life…You have this shorter-dated RPO because of some of the M365 stuff. If you look at the Azure only, RPO is a little bit more extended.

And since I’ve already modeled an OpenAI-specific risk, I don’t feel the need to model more related to CapEx. However, I also increased my WACC a little bit due to a lower free cash flow, which gives them lower financial flexibility. I still modeled that flexibility, but lower than in my previous model.

Finally, an economic recession or stagnation would obviously influence more exposed business segments: ads, gaming, and part of Azure related to small- and midsize customers, maybe about 30%-40% of their business. Let’s model two years of relatively moderate recession: FY’29 with revenue growth of 8% (instead of 14%) and FY’30 with revenue growth of 7% (instead of 12%). In this case, Fair Value would be $496 (with the initial WACC and growth in perpetuity), leaving Microsoft fairly valued.

For a GARP (Growth At a Reasonable Price) stock, I usually initiate a position when the sum Undervaluation + Revenue growth + Free Cash Flow yield is greater than Uncertainty Threshold.

For Uncertainty Threshold, I use the following values:

  • About 35% for a medium uncertainty.
  • About 50% for a high uncertainty.
  • About 75% for a very high uncertainty.

For Microsoft, I used to see a medium uncertainty, with an inherently higher uncertainty in software offset by a strong economic moat, with quasi-monopolies in Office and Windows and with multiple moat sources for Azure. However, I add about 5% due to their very high reliance on OpenAI and another 5% due to their very high CapEx concentrated in short-lived assets, for a 45% uncertainty threshold.

With the sum of 36% (undervaluation) + 15.5% (forward revenue growth) + 1.5% (free cash flow yield) = 53%, Microsoft is above my threshold; therefore, I rate it a Buy.

Strategy: What Went Well And What Could Be Better

Like mentioned in the introduction, I favored (and I still do) a bull Put Spread strategy: short Put options, long lower strike Put options. This strategy has a capped potential but performs much better with a stagnant, modestly increasing, or even slightly decreasing stock.

Let’s see how my initial position performed:
